title: Misleading Truth
description:
The function `is_positive(n)` should return `True` if `n` is strictly greater than zero, and `False` for zero and all negative numbers.

It works correctly for positive numbers and for zero. But it **incorrectly returns `True` for negative numbers**.

In Python, many values are considered "truthy" — but truthy and positive are not the same thing.
hints: Try `is_positive(-5)`. What does it return? What should it return? Now try `is_positive(-0.1)`. Same problem?
`if n:` evaluates to `True` for any non-zero number — including `-5`, `-100`, `-0.001`. "Non-zero" is not the same as "positive".
Replace the truthiness check with an actual comparison: `return n > 0`. Only use `if n:` when you want to check for any non-empty, non-zero, non-None value — not when you mean "greater than zero".
